title: Tuberculosis treatment success rate (% of new cases)
definition: Tuberculosis treatment success rate is the percentage of all new tuberculosis cases (or new and relapse cases for some countries) registered under a national tuberculosis control programme in a given year that successfully completed treatment, with or without bacteriological evidence of success ("cured" and "treatment completed" respectively).
unit: percent of new and relapse cases

source.license: Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International unless specifically labeled otherwise by World Bank metadata
source.sourceOrganization: Global Tuberculosis Report, World Health Organization (WHO)

editorial.patternNote: The darkest countries include West Bank and Gaza, Kuwait, Tanzania, while the lightest include Latvia, France, New Caledonia; that contrast is the fastest way to read the tb treatment success pattern.
editorial.whyItMatters: TB treatment success adds a health-system performance clue that differs from raw disease burden.

bestProbeCountries.reason: High anchor: this country sits near the darkest end of the map.
Low anchor: this country clarifies the lightest end of the scale.
Middle check: this country helps separate a true pattern from one-off outliers.

commonConfusions.reason: Health spending per person lives in the same category, so it can feel plausible until the high and low countries are checked.
GNI per capita overlaps with part of the visible pattern, but the full country spread does not match.
Air pollution mortality lives in the same category, so it can feel plausible until the high and low countries are checked.

editorial.difficultyReason: Expert difficulty: the map can be mistaken for adjacent indicators unless players probe a high, low, and middle country.
editorial.dataCaveat: Country reporting and case mix can affect year-to-year comparability.
regionalSignals: Health maps often separate income, public-health capacity, and conflict exposure.
Check both high-income and low-income anchors.

reviewNotes: Batch 4 QA: source-valid health-system outcome map with good coverage.
Expert-only because reporting, case mix, and disease burden can make interpretation subtle.
